agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Decompose the courier platform monolith into 5 bounded contexts mapped to 6 deployable services, prioritizing deployment independence between the highest-change-rate components (payments, location tracking). The decomposition follows domain boundaries rather than technical layers, uses asynchronous domain events for cross-service communication where possible, and preserves synchronous calls only where transactional consistency is unavoidable. For a team of 8 with moderate operational maturity, this is the maximum safe decomposition — more services would exceed operational capacity.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Order lifecycle: placement, status transitions, cancellation. Owns the Order aggregate including line items, delivery address, and order state machine. Orchestrates the order fulfillment saga (payment capture → courier assignment → delivery confirmation).","boundaries":"Owns orders table and order_events. No direct access to payment or courier data — communicates via events and synchronous API calls for saga steps."},{"name":"Courier Service","responsibility":"Courier profiles, availability management, assignment algorithm, and route optimization. Owns courier-to-order matching logic.","boundaries":"Owns courier_profiles, courier_availability, and assignments tables. Consumes location data from Location Service via internal API but does not own location storage."},{"name":"Location Service","responsibility":"Ingests, stores, and serves real-time courier GPS coordinates. Handles 5,000 writes/sec at peak. Provides current-position queries and short-term trajectory for the Courier Service routing algorithm.","boundaries":"Owns location_updates time-series store. Write-optimized, append-only. Exposes read API for current/recent positions. No business logic beyond geo-indexing and TTL-based cleanup."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Payment intent creation, charge capture, refund processing. Integrates with payment gateway (Stripe/Adyen). Enforces strict transactional consistency with idempotency keys and exactly-once semantics.","boundaries":"Owns payment_transactions, refunds, and idempotency_keys tables. Isolated database with no shared access. Emits PaymentCaptured, PaymentFailed, RefundProcessed events."},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Fan-out of customer-facing notifications across email, SMS, and push channels. Template rendering, delivery tracking, rate limiting, and channel preference management.","boundaries":"Owns notification_log and templates. Subscribes to domain events from all other services. Stateless processing — no business decisions, pure delivery."},{"name":"Customer Service","responsibility":"Customer account CRUD, authentication, address book, preferences, and profile management.","boundaries":"Owns customers, addresses, and auth_credentials tables. Provides customer lookup API consumed by Order and Notification services."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Order Service","to":"Payment Service","contract":"POST /payments/capture {order_id, amount, currency, idempotency_key} → {payment_id, status}. Synchronous — Order Service blocks on payment result before confirming order.","coupling_type":"synchronous-request"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Courier Service","contract":"Domain event: OrderPaid {order_id, pickup_location, dropoff_location, time_constraints}. Courier Service consumes asynchronously and emits CourierAssigned or AssignmentFailed.","coupling_type":"async-event"},{"from":"Courier Service","to":"Location Service","contract":"GET /locations/current?courier_ids=x,y,z → [{courier_id, lat, lng, timestamp}]. Synchronous read for assignment algorithm — needs fresh positions.","coupling_type":"synchronous-query"},{"from":"Location Service","to":"Location Service","contract":"POST /locations/batch {updates: [{courier_id, lat, lng, timestamp}]}. Courier mobile app posts GPS updates every 10 seconds. Batch endpoint reduces connection overhead.","coupling_type":"client-ingest"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Domain events: OrderConfirmed, OrderPickedUp, OrderDelivered, OrderCancelled. Notification Service subscribes and renders appropriate templates per channel.","coupling_type":"async-event"},{"from":"Payment Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Domain events: PaymentCaptured, RefundProcessed. Triggers payment confirmation and refund notification emails.","coupling_type":"async-event"},{"from":"Courier Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"Domain events: CourierAssigned, CourierNearby. Triggers ETA and arrival notifications.","coupling_type":"async-event"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Customer Service","contract":"GET /customers/{id} → {name, email, phone, default_address}. Synchronous lookup during order placement for validation and autofill.","coupling_type":"synchronous-query"}],"data_flow":"1) Customer places order → Order Service validates via Customer Service, creates order in PENDING state. 2) Order Service calls Payment Service synchronously to capture payment. On success, emits OrderPaid event. 3) Courier Service consumes OrderPaid, queries Location Service for nearby available couriers, runs assignment algorithm, emits CourierAssigned. 4) Courier mobile app streams GPS to Location Service at 10s intervals. 5) Order Service tracks delivery state transitions (picked_up, en_route, delivered) driven by courier-side events. 6) Notification Service subscribes to all domain events and fans out to appropriate channels. 7) Refunds flow: Order Service requests refund via Payment Service synchronously, Payment Service emits RefundProcessed.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Saga (Orchestration)","rationale":"Order fulfillment spans Payment and Courier assignment. The Order Service orchestrates the saga — it's the natural coordinator since it owns the order lifecycle. Orchestration over choreography because the team is new to distributed systems and orchestration is easier to debug and reason about."},{"pattern":"Event-Driven Communication","rationale":"Cross-service notifications, courier assignment triggers, and status updates use async domain events via a message broker. This decouples deployment and allows independent scaling. Location tracking and notification delivery are naturally async consumers."},{"pattern":"Database-per-Service","rationale":"Each service owns its datastore. Critical for the stated goal of deployment independence. Payment Service gets its own Postgres with strict ACID. Location Service gets a time-series store (TimescaleDB or InfluxDB) optimized for 5K writes/sec."},{"pattern":"Strangler Fig (Migration)","rationale":"Do not rewrite from scratch. Extract services one at a time from the monolith, starting with Location and Payment (highest deployment independence value). Route traffic via an API gateway that can split between monolith and new services."},{"pattern":"Idempotent Receiver","rationale":"All event consumers and the Payment Service API are idempotent. Essential for at-least-once delivery guarantees in async messaging and for safe payment retries."},{"pattern":"CQRS (Location Service only)","rationale":"Location Service separates the high-throughput write path (GPS ingestion) from the read path (current position queries). Write path is append-only to time-series storage; read path serves from a materialized current-position cache (Redis)."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Order Management","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: order_id — contains line items, delivery details, state machine)", "DeliveryWindow (value object — pickup/dropoff time constraints)"]},{"name":"Courier Operations","classification":"core","aggregates":["Courier (root: courier_id — profile, availability, vehicle type)", "Assignment (root: assignment_id — links courier to order, tracks assignment state)"]},{"name":"Location Tracking","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["CourierPosition (root: courier_id — current lat/lng/timestamp, append-only history)"]},{"name":"Payments","classification":"core","aggregates":["PaymentTransaction (root: payment_id — amount, status, gateway_reference, idempotency_key)", "Refund (entity under PaymentTransaction — partial/full refund state)"]},{"name":"Notifications","classification":"generic","aggregates":["NotificationRequest (root: notification_id — channel, recipient, template, delivery_status)"]},{"name":"Customer Identity","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["Customer (root: customer_id — profile, contact info, preferences)", "Address (value object — street, city, coordinates)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Courier Operations","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Order Management publishes OrderPaid events; Courier Operations consumes them to trigger assignment. Order Management defines the event schema; Courier Operations conforms."},{"upstream":"Courier Operations","downstream":"Location Tracking","relationship":"Customer-Supplier. Courier Operations queries Location Tracking for current positions. Location Tracking provides a stable query API; Courier Operations consumes it."},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Payments","relationship":"Customer-Supplier with ACL. Order Management calls Payment Service but translates between its order-centric model and the payment-centric model via an anti-corruption layer in the Order Service."},{"upstream":"Order Management","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Conformist. Notifications conforms to domain events published by Order Management (and other contexts). No influence on upstream schemas."},{"upstream":"Courier Operations","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Conformist. Same pattern — Notifications subscribes to courier events without influencing their shape."},{"upstream":"Customer Identity","downstream":"Order Management","relationship":"Shared Kernel (minimal). Customer ID and basic contact info are shared types. Kept minimal to limit coupling."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"6 services for 8 engineers (roughly 1.3 engineers per service)","gains":"Clear ownership, independent deployment of highest-value components (payments, location), right-sized team-to-service ratio","sacrifices":"Some services will share on-call rotation, less specialization per service than ideal","alternatives_considered":["Fewer services (3-4): lower operational burden but doesn't achieve deployment independence between payments and location","More services (8+): exceeds team's operational capacity at current maturity level"]},{"decision":"Orchestrated saga over choreography for order fulfillment","gains":"Easier to debug, explicit workflow visible in Order Service, simpler failure handling and compensation","sacrifices":"Order Service becomes a coordination point — slightly higher coupling than pure choreography","alternatives_considered":["Choreography-based saga: more decoupled but significantly harder to trace, debug, and handle partial failures for a team new to distributed systems"]},{"decision":"Synchronous call for payment capture (Order → Payment)","gains":"Strict consistency — customer gets immediate payment confirmation or failure. Simpler error handling during order placement.","sacrifices":"Runtime coupling between Order and Payment services during order creation. Payment Service downtime blocks order placement.","alternatives_considered":["Async payment with eventual consistency: decoupled but creates confusing UX where order is placed but payment might fail later. Unacceptable for a payment-critical flow."]},{"decision":"Dedicated time-series store for Location Service","gains":"Handles 5K writes/sec comfortably, natural fit for GPS data with TTL, efficient geo-queries","sacrifices":"Additional operational burden — team must learn and operate a time-series database","alternatives_considered":["Postgres with partitioning: simpler ops but will struggle at 5K writes/sec sustained without significant tuning","Redis Streams only: fast writes but poor durability and limited query capability for historical traces"]},{"decision":"Strangler Fig migration over big-bang rewrite","gains":"Incremental risk, can validate each service in production before extracting the next, monolith continues serving traffic during transition","sacrifices":"Longer total migration timeline (6-12 months), temporary complexity of running hybrid architecture","alternatives_considered":["Big-bang rewrite: faster if it works, but 3 years of monolith logic makes this extremely risky. Industry failure rate for big-bang rewrites is very high."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Notification channels","evolution_path":"Notification Service is channel-agnostic internally. Adding new channels (WhatsApp, in-app) requires only a new channel adapter — no changes to upstream services."},{"area":"Courier assignment algorithm","evolution_path":"Assignment logic is encapsulated in Courier Service. Can evolve from simple nearest-available to ML-based optimization without affecting any other service."},{"area":"Location Service storage backend","evolution_path":"Behind a repository abstraction. Can migrate from TimescaleDB to InfluxDB or a managed service without affecting consumers — only the internal storage layer changes."},{"area":"Payment gateway","evolution_path":"Payment Service wraps the gateway behind an adapter interface. Switching from Stripe to Adyen (or supporting multiple gateways) is isolated to this service."},{"area":"Event broker","evolution_path":"Start with a managed broker (Amazon SNS+SQS, Google Pub/Sub) to minimize ops burden. Can migrate to Kafka later if event replay or stream processing becomes necessary."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Order state machine","reason":"The order lifecycle (pending → paid → assigned → picked_up → delivered / cancelled) is the core business invariant. Changes here cascade to saga logic, event schemas, and notification triggers. This should change slowly and deliberately."},{"area":"Payment idempotency contract","reason":"The idempotency key mechanism and exactly-once semantics are foundational to financial correctness. This contract must be rigid — breaking it risks double charges or lost payments."},{"area":"Domain event schemas","reason":"Published event schemas are contracts between services. Must be versioned and backward-compatible. Breaking changes require coordinated migration across consumers."},{"area":"Customer ID as shared identifier","reason":"Customer ID is referenced across Order, Payment, Notification, and Customer services. Changing its format or semantics requires coordinated migration everywhere."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Operational maturity gap: team has 3 years of monolith experience but zero distributed systems production experience. Debugging distributed failures, managing multiple deployments, handling partial outages — all new.","severity":"critical","mitigation":"1) Extract only 2 services initially (Location + Payment — highest independence value). Run them alongside the monolith for 3+ months before extracting more. 2) Invest in observability from day one: distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry), centralized logging, service mesh health checks. 3) Budget 20% of sprint capacity for operational tooling during migration."},{"risk":"Data consistency across services during migration: the monolith's single database enforces cross-domain consistency via transactions. Splitting databases means eventual consistency in some flows.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Identify all cross-domain transactions in the monolith before splitting. For each, decide: saga, synchronous call, or accept eventual consistency. Document the consistency guarantees of each flow explicitly."},{"risk":"Location Service write throughput: 5,000 writes/sec is achievable but requires correct storage choice and write-path optimization. Misconfiguration leads to data loss or backpressure on courier apps.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Use a time-series database (TimescaleDB on Postgres or InfluxDB). Batch writes from courier apps (buffer 3-5 updates, send in one request). Load test to 2x peak (10K writes/sec) before go-live."},{"risk":"Saga failure and compensation: if payment succeeds but courier assignment fails, the system must handle compensation (refund or retry). Incorrect saga implementation leads to stuck orders or orphaned payments.","severity":"high","mitigation":"Implement saga with explicit state persistence in Order Service. Each step has a compensating action. Add a dead-letter mechanism for failed saga steps with alerting. Test failure scenarios explicitly in integration tests."},{"risk":"Event ordering and duplication: async events may arrive out of order or be delivered multiple times. Consumers that assume ordering or exactly-once delivery will have subtle bugs.","severity":"medium","mitigation":"All consumers must be idempotent. Use event sequence numbers within an aggregate for ordering. Design consumers to handle out-of-order delivery gracefully (e.g., ignore events older than current state)."},{"risk":"Premature extraction of too many services at once","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Strict phased approach: Phase 1 extracts Location + Payment only. Phase 2 (after 3 months of stable operation) extracts Courier + Notification. Phase 3 extracts Customer. Order Service may remain the last to leave the monolith since it orchestrates everything."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Strangler Fig migration in 3 phases over 9-12 months. Phase 1 (months 1-3): Extract Location Service and Payment Service. These have the clearest boundaries, highest deployment independence value, and most distinct technical requirements. Place an API gateway in front of the monolith to route /locations/* and /payments/* to new services while everything else stays in the monolith. Phase 2 (months 4-7): Extract Courier Service and Notification Service. By now the team has operational experience with 2 services. Phase 3 (months 8-12): Extract Customer Service and refactor the remaining monolith into Order Service. Introduce the event broker in Phase 1 — even if the first two services don't heavily event-communicate, having the broker infrastructure ready is essential for Phase 2.","files_to_create_or_modify":["docs/adr/001-service-decomposition.md — this architecture decision record","docs/adr/002-location-service-storage.md — time-series store selection rationale","docs/adr/003-saga-pattern-order-fulfillment.md — saga design for order lifecycle","docs/context-map.md — visual bounded context map with relationships","docs/migration-plan.md — phased Strangler Fig plan with rollback criteria","docs/event-catalog.md — schema registry for all domain events with versioning policy","infra/docker-compose.yml — local development environment with all services","services/location-service/ — first service to extract","services/payment-service/ — second service to extract (Phase 1)","shared/events/ — published language for domain event schemas (lightweight shared kernel)"],"patterns_to_follow":"Each service follows hexagonal architecture internally: domain core with no framework dependencies, ports (interfaces) for inbound and outbound, adapters for HTTP/messaging/database. This keeps each service testable in isolation and allows infrastructure swaps without touching domain logic. Use contract testing (Pact) between services to catch integration breaks before deployment. Use feature flags to gradually shift traffic from monolith to new services.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Distributed monolith: if services share a database or make synchronous calls for every operation, you get the worst of both worlds — distributed complexity with monolithic coupling. Enforce database-per-service from day one.","Extracting too many services at once: the team will be overwhelmed by operational complexity. Strict 2-service-at-a-time extraction.","Skipping observability investment: without distributed tracing and centralized logging, debugging production issues across services is nearly impossible. This is not optional — it's prerequisite infrastructure.","Over-engineering event schemas: start with simple JSON events and a lightweight schema registry. Do not invest in Avro/Protobuf schema evolution until you have proven the need.","Shared libraries that grow into coupling: the shared events package must contain ONLY event schema definitions. No business logic, no utilities, no shared models beyond event contracts.","Ignoring the monolith during migration: the monolith will continue to evolve during the 9-12 month migration. Ensure new monolith features respect the emerging service boundaries to avoid creating new cross-boundary coupling that will need to be untangled later."]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Service boundary identification — these align with clear domain boundaries and the team's stated deployment independence goals","Location Service technical approach — time-series workload with CQRS is well-established pattern for GPS tracking","Payment Service isolation — strict consistency requirements make this an obvious independent service","Strangler Fig migration strategy — industry-proven approach, especially appropriate for a team with no distributed systems experience"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact throughput capacity of TimescaleDB vs InfluxDB for this workload — would need benchmarking with representative data","Team's actual operational readiness — moderate maturity is subjective; the phased approach mitigates but doesn't eliminate risk","Whether 6 services is the right final count — Customer Service could arguably stay merged with Order Service if the team finds 6 too many to operate"],"assumptions":["The 8 engineers are full-stack and can own services end-to-end (if frontend/backend split, team-to-service mapping changes)","There is budget for managed infrastructure (message broker, time-series DB) rather than self-hosting everything","The monolith is in a language/framework that supports the Strangler Fig pattern (can route at the HTTP level)","Peak of 5,000 location writes/sec is a measured number, not a guess — architecture depends on this being accurate"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"Decompose the courier platform monolith into 5 bounded contexts mapped to 6 deployable services, prioritizing deployment independence between the highest-change-rate components (payments, location tracking). The decomposition follows domain boundaries rather than technical layers, uses asynchronous domain events for cross-service communication where possible, and preserves synchronous calls only where transactional consistency is unavoidable.
